title: Advanced Customization via the NIMBLE MCMC Engine
text: Now, execute NIMBLE engine manually. explicitly, freedom intercept configuration object apply custom block samplers change hyperparameters calling nimbleMCMC(). Note sake testing/tutorial, code defaults fast compilation format. rigorous real-world analysis, migh run 10,000 iterations across 2-3 chains! Understanding Execution Output enabled summary WAIC flags function call , nimbleMCMC() conclude sampling process return composite list mcmc_raw object containing three primary elements: samples: mcmc.list containing raw, point--point posterior draws every tracked parameter across chains. summary: pre-calculated matrix providing mean, median, standard deviation, key credible intervals every estimated parameter. WAIC (Watanabe-Akaike Information Criterion): estimator --sample predictive accuracy. Lower WAIC values indicate better-fitting model, highly useful comparing competing Q-Matrices network architectures. pWAIC: estimated “effective number parameters.” complex hierarchical Bayesian networks like cognitive modeling, penalty term describes structural complexity shrinkage model, penalizing overly parameterized networks risk overfitting.
code: mcmc_raw <- nimbleMCMC(     code = model_code, # The NIMBLE code we extracted earlier     constants = config$constants, # Fixed constants mapped from the Q-Matrix     data = config$data, # The isolated observational vectors     inits = config$inits, # Starter values for the Markov chain     monitors = config$monitors, # Variables we want NIMBLE to track     nchains = 2,     niter = 2000,     nburnin = 500,     summary = TRUE,     samplesAsCodaMCMC = TRUE, # Force output to a list format compatible with CODA     WAIC = TRUE # Calculate the Watanabe-Akaike Information Criterion )

Extract original participant IDs for correct row mapping student_ids <- rownames(config$data$X)  # 3. Map Nimble parameters to human-readable names mapped_results <- map_pgdcm_parameters(     summary_mx = mcmc_summ,     config_obj = config,     student_names = student_ids )  # 4. Generate the final clean tables (Skill Profiles, Item Parameters) summary_tables <- generate_summary_tables(     mapped_results = mapped_results,     config_obj = config,     student_names = student_ids,     return_groups = TRUE # Enable this to explicitly extract skill profile clusters )  # Extract the skill profiles specifically skill_profiles <- summary_tables$skill_profiles group_patterns <- summary_tables$group_patterns # Assess classification accuracy against known true profiles accuracy_results <- assess_classification_accuracy(     skill_profiles = skill_profiles,     true_data = dtmr_true_profiles,     mapping_list = list(         "referent_units" = "referent_units",         "partitioning_iterating" = "partitioning_iterating",         "appropriateness" = "appropriateness",         "multiplicative_comparison" = "multiplicative_comparison"     ) )  # View the individual skill accuracies, Cohen's Kappa, and overall profile match rate print(accuracy_results$metrics) print(paste("Profile Correct Classification Rate:", accuracy_results$profile_accuracy))

what: 2. Preparing the Data and the Evidence Model
title: Beginner Tutorial: Introduction to Bayesian Modeling with PGDCM
text: Diagnostic Classification Models (DCMs) evaluate whether participants mastered specific skills assessment designed measure. estimate , model requires two key components: Observational Data (X): actual test responses participants, typically consisting 1s (correct) 0s (incorrect). forms N×JN \times J matrix, NN number participants JJ number questions (items). Q-Matrix (Q): evidence model specifying exact skills required answer question correctly. forms J×KJ \times K matrix, JJ number questions KK number skills (attributes). Specifically, entry 1 jj-th row kk-th column indicates kk-th skill required answer jj-th question, 0 indicates . Let’s load sample dataset Q-Matrix: pgdcm package mathematically treats modern psychometric models probabilistic graphical models (specifically, Directed Acyclic Graphs Bayesian Networks). , must first convert standard Q-Matrix Directed Acyclic Graph (g) represents underlying node dependencies—.e., dependencies skills tasks/test items. QMatrix2iGraph() function handles graphical conversion automatically us. constructed core network graph, several model parameters must defined establish active modeling environment NIMBLE. includes validating topological constraints, isolating constants required nimble inference calculations, setting initial values model parameters. build_model_config() function handles heavy lifting. important note default, build_model_config() assumes DINA (Deterministic Input, Noisy “” gate) compute type. implies strict, non-compensatory cognitive framework—meaning student must possess required skills dictated Q-Matrix likely answer item correctly; mastering just one required skills provides additional benefit. assessment follows different theoretic framework, can overwrite supplying options like compute = "dino" compute = "dinm": compute = "dino" (Deterministic Input, Noisy “” gate): Assumes fully compensatory framework. , possessing least one required skills sufficient likely answer item correctly. compute = "dinm" (Deterministic Input, Noisy “Mixed”): Assumes proportional additive framework. model, additional required skill student masters incrementally increases probability answering correctly.
code: X <- dtmr_data Q <- dtmr_qmatrix # Restructure Matrix to an iGraph object g <- QMatrix2iGraph(Q)  # Link student responses to the Graph config <- build_model_config(g, X)

MCMC algorithms take time traverse towards high-probability region; discarding early “burn-” samples ensures analyze parameters reached stable state. chains = 2: algorithm runs two independent sampling processes simultaneously ensure converge onto distribution. Additionally, standard comprehensive workflows evaluate viability model using simulation. run_pgdcm_auto arguments (prior_sims = NULL, post_sims = NULL) bypass default speed, critical components full Bayesian workflow: Prior Predictive Checking: seeing actual data, kind data model think see based purely initial parameter bounds (priors)? ensures initial limits logical mathematically impossible. This prefix is used while saving     # the prior predictive and posterior predictive simulation results. )  # Save the exact results to an RDS file to bypass future recomputation saveRDS(results, "Beginner_Tutorial_Results.rds")

text: Unlike standard frequentist statistics often output single “best guess” point estimate, Bayesian inference MCMC outputs thousands plausible values drawn joint posterior distribution. fundamental difference allows us naturally extract comprehensive metrics like posterior mean credible intervals formally quantify uncertainty. easily read interpret distributions, load cached .rds file use MCMCvis package. passing raw MCMC samples MCMCsummary() function, can collapse complex distributions clean summary table provides point estimates measures numerical uncertainty. lambda parameters model capture properties test items . test item jj: lambda[j, 1] (Discrimination / Slope): Represents effectively item distinguishes participants possess required skills . higher value indicates item strongly differentiates mastery states. lambda[j, 2] (Difficulty / Intercept): Represents baseline difficulty item. higher value typically means question inherently harder answer correctly overall. (also parameter families estimated graphical model capture attribute network . example, beta_root parameters represent baseline prevalence foundational “root” skills population. theta parameters function similarly lambda, applied higher-level skills, carrying dependencies baseline difficulties.) can extract numerical summary matrix specifically lambda parameters inspect test items functioned: Read Table: mean: expected value best estimate parameter. conceptually corresponds traditional point estimate obtain frequentist approach. 2.5% 97.5%: bounds define 95% Credible Interval. Bayesian inference, means 95% probability true underlying parameter value falls within specific range. Rhat: convergence diagnostic (Gelman-Rubin statistic). Rhat greater 1.1, generally indicates MCMC chains yet converged, may need substantially increase niter (number iterations) estimation step. Sometimes Rhat values remain greater 1.1 due underlying model misspecification. issue can typically identified continue increase number iterations observe improvement convergence diagnostics. Reviewing hundreds rows table can exhausting. MCMC visualization functions like MCMCplot() can transform parameter boundaries intuitive visual “Caterpillar Plots”. Note: estimated 54 item parameters dataset, plotting simultaneously can vertically cramp screen. Standard R plotting packages (like MCMCvis) may auto-truncate Y-axis labels fit visual constraints, occasionally dropping text labels save space.  Interpret Visuals: dot represents mean estimate specific parameter. horizontal lines stretching outwards illustrate 95% Credible Interval. wider line indicates high uncertainty (broader credible interval) parameter. shorter, narrower line indicates model highly confident estimate (tighter credible interval) parameter. text: vignette demonstrates new unified architecture running DCM model. Note DCM subtypes (HigherOrder, BayesNet, Traditional, MIRT, IRT) automatically determined.
code: # Activate the local renv if it isn't automatically loaded: # renv::activate()  # Load the package and Cytoscape interface library(pgdcm) library(RCy3)  # Helper to find built-in package data get_data_path <- function(filename) {     system.file("extdata", filename, package = "pgdcm") } print("Building Graph from Q-Matrix...") graph_res <- build_from_q_matrix(     QMatrixFile = get_data_path("Q_dummy.csv"),     DefaultAttributeCompute = "dina", # discrete DCM     DefaultTaskCompute = "dina" ) print("Creating DCM Model Configuration...")  DataDF <- read.csv(get_data_path("Data_dummy.csv"))  config <- build_model_config(graph_res, DataDF) print(paste("Configured Model Type:", config$type)) print("Starting Integrated Workflow...")  estimation_config <- list(     niter = 500,     nburnin = 100,     chains = 2,     prior_sims = 50,     post_sims = 50 )  results <- run_pgdcm_auto(     config = config,     estimation_config = estimation_config,     prefix = "Unified_DCM_Example" )
